# Crisis & Opportunity

> A digital repository of early-20th-century sociology works by Black scholars (1892-1940). Sixty-one articles totaling roughly 261,000 words, hand-curated from journals such as *The American Journal of Sociology*, *Opportunity*, *Social Forces*, *The Annals*, and *The Southern Workman*. All texts are in the public domain. Each article is available as HTML, plain markdown, and (where available) a PDF scan of the original.

Authors include W.E.B. Du Bois, Ida B. Wells, Anna J. Cooper, Kelly Miller, Monroe N. Work, Charles S. Johnson, E. Franklin Frazier, Abram L. Harris, Ira De A. Reid, Elizabeth Ross Haynes, Elise Johnson McDougald, Fannie Barrier Williams, Horace Mann Bond, Ralph J. Bunche, and others.

## Site

- [About](https://crisisopportunity.org/about.html): project background and scope
- [Full article index](https://crisisopportunity.org/index.html): human-readable index of all articles
- [Artwork credits](https://crisisopportunity.org/art.html): Harlem Renaissance artwork used throughout the site
- [Sitemap](https://crisisopportunity.org/sitemap.xml): machine-readable sitemap index

## Racial Identity

- [Is the American Negro to Remain Black?](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/bleached.md): Kelly Miller (1926), *South Atlantic Quarterly*.
  Miller argues that despite twenty-six states' anti-miscegenation laws and the Nordic purity propaganda sweeping 1920s America, the white sexual exploitation of Black women has already produced a new mulatto majority whose physical destiny is a yellowish-brown Negroid type rather than absorption into whiteness. Reading the 1890, 1910, and 1920 censuses on mulatto counts alongside legal history and observations of Black schools and churches, he contends that a coherent Black racial consciousness must be built on this mixed physical foundation.
- [Racial Self-Expression](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/selfexpression.md): E. Franklin Frazier (1927), *Ebony and Topaz: A Collectanea*.
  Intervening in the Hughes-Schuyler debate and the broader Harlem Renaissance quarrel over whether Black artists should pursue a unique racial culture, Frazier argues that the Negro's cultural situation is unlike any other American nationality: stripped of African tradition by slavery, he has no old-world reservoir to draw on and must participate in American culture or face arrested development. Drawing on anthropology's dismantling of racial-temperament theories and reading James Weldon Johnson's God's Trombones as a model, he endorses group tradition-building only insofar as it enables fuller participation rather than isolation.
- [Two Racial Islands in Alabama](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/two_islands.md): Horace Mann Bond (1931), *American Journal of Sociology*.
  Contact between a dominant group and a subordinate group results, through miscegenation, in a third group midway between the two parent-stocks. This third group seeks identification with the dominant group, although the latter may deny such identification. At the same time, because of the characteristics identifying it with the dominant group, it protests against identification with the subordinate group, to which it holds itself superior, and it achieves a status much above that occupied by the subordinate parent-group. This formula of race, which is descriptive of many situations, may be applied with exactitude to two racial islands in America, the Creoles and the Cajuns, both in Alabama. Although quite different in social traits and qualities---in industry, in thrift and cleanliness, in social organization, in intellectual ability, in culture---these two groups are alike in having to occupy distinctive social positions, on the one hand disclaimed by their white parent-groups and on the other hand themselves disclaiming their Negro parents. They equally demonstrate the applicability of the formula of race in America.

## White Racism and Racial Violence

- [Has America a Race Problem](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/race_problem.md): Anna J. Cooper (1892), *A Voice from the South*.
  Cooper argues that racial and cultural diversity, rather than threatening American civilization, is the very engine of its progress, drawing on a comparative reading of Asiatic, Greek, and European history by way of Guizot to show that societies governed by a single dominant principle stagnate while those marked by conflict advance. She rejects amalgamation, deportation, and colonization schemes and insists that the Black population is an indispensable element in America's equilibrium of contending forces.
- [Economic Aspects of Race Prejudice](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/economic_aspects.md): W.E.B. Du Bois (1910), *The Editorial Review*.
  Du Bois reframes race prejudice as an economic liability rather than a matter of personal taste, arguing it distorts U.S. democracy through Southern disfranchisement, depresses wages by pitting white and Black workers against each other, and handicaps American trade with Asia, Africa, and Latin America. Citing the Atlanta riot and demagogic Southern politics as symptoms, he calls for reviving the Blair bill to fund common schools as the first corrective.
- [The Negro Problem](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/negro_problem.md): Richard R. Wright, Jr. (1911), *The Negro Problem*.
  Being extracts from two lectures on "The Sociological Point Of View In The Study Of Race Problems," and  "The Negro Problem; What It Is Not, And What It Is"
- [Public Opinion and the Negro](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/public_opinion.md): Charles S. Johnson (1923), *Opportunity*.
  Johnson anatomizes the "pictures in our heads" that structure white American opinion about Black people, identifying three cardinal beliefs— innate mental inferiority, constitutional immorality, and natural criminality—and tracing each to its pseudo-scientific sources, from Charles Carroll's biblical arguments and Bean's discredited brain-weight study to the Army intelligence tests administered disproportionately in the Beta form. Drawing on Lippmann, Mill, and the Chicago race riot, he argues these stereotypes distort Black conduct and rationalize mob violence from Washington to East St. Louis.
- [Taking Stock of the Race Problem](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/stock.md): Monroe N. Work (1923), *Opportunity*.
  Work surveys census and mortality data through 1923 to argue that Black Americans are making measurable gains in population health, literacy, landownership, and urban concentration even as disfranchisement and Jim Crow persist. Marshaling tables on death rates, property accumulation, and the ten Northern industrial districts absorbing migrants, he recasts the race problem as national rather than sectional and reframes interracial cooperation as a shift from accommodation to assimilation.
- [Review of The Negro in Chicago,](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/negro_in_chicago.md): Kelly Miller (1924), *American Journal of Sociology*.
  Miller reviews the Chicago Commission on Race Relations' 1922 report on the 1919 riot, praising it as the first such study produced by a genuinely co-equal biracial commission and treating its findings as generalizable to Northern cities absorbing migrants. He highlights the volume's analysis of housing segregation, labor competition, and press incitement, while faulting its evasion of the school question and arguing that residential separation will prove both cause and partial cure of race friction.
- [Economic Foundations of American Race Division](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/foundations.md): Abram L. Harris (1927), *Social Forces*.
  Harris argues that American race antipathy is not rooted in innate prejudice or cultural difference but in the economic relations of slavery, which hardened class exploitation into caste and left a stereotype-laden public opinion as its residue. Drawing on colonial court records, Phillips's history of slave prices, Jefferson and Calhoun's pro-slavery dogmas, and an original questionnaire of 144 prominent whites on Black mental traits, he shows how contradictory "scientific" judgments about Black capacity continue to rationalize a caste order the Civil War failed to dissolve.
- [The Pathology of Race Prejudice](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/pathology.md): E. Franklin Frazier (1927), *The Forum*.
  The attitude of races to one another has given rise to much speculation, and many writers have maintained that men do not differ greatly from ants in their antipathies. Race hatred is so strong among ants that their battles can be arranged with certainty by entomologists for the movies. A more flattering view of human prejudices is here suggested; for if they are chiefly due to psychopathology there is hope that with the progress of science a somewhat more rational attitude may eventually prevail.

## Great Migration and Urban Sociology

- [Social Bonds in the "Black Belt" of Chicago](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/social_bonds.md): Fannie Barrier Williams (1905), *Charities*.
  Williams maps the associational life of Chicago's rapidly growing Black population—roughly 50,000 in 1905, up forty percent in five years—and argues that residential segregation in the Black Belt has forced the community to build its own dense infrastructure of churches, secret orders, and clubs. She catalogs twenty-five churches, fourteen Odd Fellows and Masonic lodges, and new settlement ventures like the Frederick Douglass Center and Trinity Mission, while documenting the steady displacement of Black workers from barbering, waiting, and janitorial trades by Swedish and Greek immigrants.
- [Negroes Move North: Departure from the South](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/north_i.md): George Edmund Haynes (1918), *The Survey*.
  Haynes documents the first wave of the Great Migration—estimating 400,000 to 500,000 Black Southerners moved north in 1916–1917—and argues it was propelled jointly by boll-weevil devastation, wartime Northern labor demand, and accumulated grievances over lynching, peonage, inferior schools, and courtroom injustice. Drawing on visits to seven Southern states, migrant letters, and editorials from the Memphis Commercial-Appeal and Atlanta Constitution, he identifies three types of migrants and urges interracial local committees as the Southern remedy.
- [How Much is the Migration a Flight from Persecution?](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/flight.md): Charles S. Johnson (1923), *Opportunity*.
  Johnson argues that the Great Migration is driven less by flight from white violence than by the exhaustion of Southern soil and the pull of urban wages. Correlating lynching counts with county-level Black population change in Georgia, Alabama, and Texas between 1888 and 1918, he shows that Black populations often grew in the most violent counties while whites also left, and traces the real engine of movement to cotton tenancy, landlessness, and the lure of Northern and Southwestern cities.
- [The New Frontage of American Life](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/frontage.md): Charles S. Johnson (1925), *The New Negro An Interpretation*.
  Johnson narrates the Great Migration as the remaking of Black life along a new urban frontier, following the half-million who poured into Chicago, Pittsburgh, New York, Cleveland, and Detroit as European labor vanished during the war. Combining industrial recruitment histories, wage data, and scene-setting portraits of the destination cities, he argues that the movement is not a panicked flight but a deliberate reach for higher wages, anonymity, and the cultural excitement of Northern life.
- [Durham: Capital of the Black Middle Class](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/durham.md): E. Franklin Frazier (1925), *The New Negro An Interpretation*.
  Frazier argues that Durham, North Carolina, is producing something new in Black life—a genuine middle class organized around production rather than consumption—and that its entrepreneurs embody the capitalist psychology rather than the aristocratic or peasant dispositions historically ascribed to Black Americans. Tracing the careers of John Merrick, C. C. Spaulding, and their associates at the North Carolina Mutual Life Insurance Company, the Mechanics and Farmers Bank, and the Royal Knights of King David, he shows a second generation now inheriting a disciplined business culture.
- [Negro Migration in New York State](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/migration.md): Eugene Kinckle Jones (1926), *Opportunity*.
  Jones argues that the Great Migration has transformed New York into a laboratory of Black urban life, where economic pull—not merely Southern lynching—drives settlement patterns toward industrial centers. Drawing on census data, occupational surveys in Buffalo and New York City, and infant-mortality figures from Harlem, the National Urban League director documents gains in employment, health, and civic presence while warning that white attitudes, not Black capacity, remain the decisive test of democracy.
- [Where Is the Negro’s Heaven?](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/heaven.md): Kelly Miller (1926), *Opportunity*.
  Responding to Carl Van Vechten's Nigger Heaven and Harlem's claim to cultural preeminence, Miller argues that Washington, D.C.—not New York— remains the true capital of Black American life. Marshalling census figures, institutional inventories of Howard University, the Freedmen's Hospital, and the District's segregated-but-parallel school system, he contends that Washington's Black elite, professional self-sufficiency, and concentration of educated leadership give it a depth Harlem's jazz-age effervescence cannot match.
- [Mrs. Bailey Pays the Rent](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/rent.md): Ira De A. Reid (1927), *Ebony and Topaz: A Collectanea*.
  Reid traces the Harlem rent party from its Southern antecedents—the "struggles," "break-downs," and chitterling parties of Atlanta's Beaver Slide—to its commercialized 1920s incarnation as the "Social Whist Party," a nocturnal response to extortionate Harlem rents. Drawing on participant observation at a West 134th Street apartment, the patter of wayside-printer invitation tickets, and a New York Age account of a rent- party homicide, he reads the institution as both ingenious tenant economy and a commercialized space policed by "Home Defense Officers" and menaced by card sharks.
- [Mirrors of Harlem](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/mirrors_of_harlem.md): Ira De A. Reid (1927), *Social Forces*.
  Reid rejects the press image of Harlem as either Mecca or slum, arguing instead that it is an unstudied social laboratory where West Indian, Southern, and native New York populations collide under conditions of employment discrimination, exorbitant rents, and uneven health. Marshaling 1913 Property Owners Improvement Association circulars on property values, 1920 census occupational counts, Urban League placement data, and sanitary-district mortality rates showing Harlem infants dying at twice the Manhattan rate, he calls for an integrated research program adequate to the community's complexity.

## Labor and Economics

- [The Negro in Times of Industrial Unrest](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/unrest.md): Richard R. Wright, Jr. (1905), *Charities*.
  Drawing on six years of direct observation in Chicago, Wright reconstructs Black workers' role in the 1900 building trades, 1904 stockyards, and 1905 teamsters strikes, arguing that industrial unrest was the only door by which Black laborers entered trades otherwise closed by white unions and employers. He rejects the stereotype of Black workers as habitual strikebreakers, noting many joined unions and that union hostility, not Black preference, drove the pattern.
- [The Rural South](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/rural_south.md): W.E.B. Du Bois (1910), *American Statistical Association*.
  Paper read at the joint meeting of the American Economic Association and the American Statistical Association, Washington, D. C., December 28, 1911.
- [Economic Future of the Negro American](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/economic.md): W.E.B. Du Bois and Augustus Granville Dill (1912), *The Negro American Artisan*.
  Du Bois and Dill divide Black America into three economic strata—some 300,000 independents, roughly 1.75 million struggling artisans and tenants, and over two million common laborers—and argue that race prejudice, union exclusion, and Southern peonage block the middle group's access to national industry. Drawing on the Thirteenth Census and the Calhoun School's cooperative land-buying experiment in Lowndes County, Alabama, they contend that justice, the ballot, and common schools are preconditions for Black economic development.
- [White and Black World in American Labor and Politics](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/american_labor.md): Abram L. Harris (1925), *Social Forces*.
  Harris argues that the 1924 La Follette progressive campaign failed to win Black votes because craft unions affiliated with the AFL—including the Machinists and Railway Brotherhoods—barred Black workers, leaving Black laborers rationally attached to the Republicans and to individual employers. Drawing on labor history since the postbellum National Labor Union, union constitutions, Urban League reports, and the trajectory of the Messenger, he contends that race psychology, religious orthodoxy, and the Negro intellectual's forced preoccupation with the color line block working-class solidarity and liberal politics alike.
- [Negro Labor's Quarrel with White Workingmen](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/quarrel.md): Abram L. Harris (1926), *Current History*.
  Harris traces the American Federation of Labor's temporizing and exclusionary posture toward Black workers from the 1902 resolutions through the 1925 American Negro Labor Congress, showing how local color prejudice and silent constitutional barriers kept Black workers out even where national policy professed inclusion. Drawing on convention records, union constitutions, correspondence between Samuel Gompers and the Urban League, and the Communist-backed Chicago congress, he frames the emerging Pullman porter organizing drive as the pivot from demanding work to demanding collective power.
- [Lily-White Labor](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/lilywhite.md): Ira De A. Reid (1930), *Opportunity*.
  Reid indicts the American Federation of Labor for four decades of paper resolutions against racial exclusion while twenty-two national unions, eleven of them AFL affiliates, barred Black workers by constitution or ritual. Marshalling union constitutions, Federation convention records, and membership statistics showing Negro locals collapsing from 169 in 1919 to 21 in 1929, he argues the AFL's segregated "federal labor union" policy entrenched Jim Crow rather than dismantling it.
- [Incidence Upon the Negroes](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/incidence.md): Charles S. Johnson (1935), *American Journal of Sociology*.
  As a result of the marginal status of Negro workers both in industry and agriculture the depression began for them ahead of American workers generally. In industrial centers their unemployment rates have consistently exceeded the rate for white workers, due both to mass employment on the common labor level of those industries most seriously curtailed and to increased racial competition. Recovery programs have tended on the whole to follow regional practices with respect to types of work and relief. In many urban centers larger proportions of Negroes are permitted in the relief rolls in consideration of higher unemployment rates. In rural areas the proportions on relief and amounts spent per person have been less than the white, despite greater need.
- [Conflict of Caste and Class in an American Industry](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/caste_and_class.md): Charles S. Johnson (1936), *American Journal of Sociology*.
  Negro labor made its earliest significant shift from field to factory in the first notable industry in the South, the manufacture of tobacco. The Negro eagerly sought employment in the industry and throughout the period of slavery held high a monopoly of tobacco work establishing a fixed association of Negroes and tobacco. With each advance in machinery there has been an increase in white male and female labor, and Negro workers have held only those jobs secured to them by low wages, disagreeable dust, and by tasks regarded as too heavy for native-born white Americans. The Negro worker is by tradition to \“stay in his place,\” but his place is being continually narrowed. The race tradition with its ritual of segregation, regarded by white workers as natural and by the Negro workers as traditional, is used by the industry to keep the two groups actively and impotently in conflict. The present working relation of constant bickering and confusion does not present a solid labor front competent to bargain with capital.
- [Social Organization of the Negro Community and the Union Movement](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/social_organization.md): Horace R. Cayton and George S. Mitchell (1939), *Black Workers and the New Unions*.
  Cayton and Mitchell argue that Black workers' wariness of trade unions cannot be explained by white racism alone — it is also produced by the internal class structure of the Black community, where a professional and business elite dependent on segregated markets, together with churches and the Black press, has historically sided with employers against organized labor. Drawing on Chicago field observation and interviews with physicians, ministers, and civic leaders, they map the social terrain the CIO had to navigate to organize Black industrial workers.

## Gender

- [The Status of Woman in America](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/status.md): Anna J. Cooper (1892), *A Voice from the South*.
  Cooper contends that American civilization has entered a new era in which moral and social questions, rather than frontier conquest or wealth accumulation, will set the national agenda, and that women are uniquely positioned to shape it. Writing from the standpoint of the Black clubwoman, she argues that the colored woman faces both a woman question and a race problem at once, and sketches a lineage from Frances Harper and Sojourner Truth to Fanny Jackson Coppin to claim a distinctive vantage on the nation's moral reconstruction.
- [Two Million Negro Women at Work](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/two_million.md): Elizabeth Ross Haynes (1922), *The Southern Workman*.
  Haynes surveys the two million Black women in paid labor across domestic service, agriculture, and manufacturing, documenting the postwar rollback of wartime industrial gains as white women displaced Black workers in Detroit offices, Washington laundries, and Middle Western garment factories. Drawing on a 1918–19 Women's Bureau survey of 150 plants, wage data from Los Angeles to Alabama cotton fields, and her own correspondence with a Black Belt landowner, she exposes backless stools, sixty-hour weeks, and the urgent need for standardized domestic-service training.
- [The Task of Negro Womanhood](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/womanhood.md): Elise Johnson McDougald (1925), *The New Negro An Interpretation*.
  McDougald sorts Harlem's Black women into four occupational strata—a small leisure class, professionals and businesswomen, trade and industrial workers, and domestic and casual laborers—to argue that their problems cannot be generalized and that sexual stigma attached to Black women reflects socio-economic condition, not race. Drawing on specific Harlem workplaces, civil-service hiring, union organizing, and the sororities and clubs of the National Association of Colored Women, she insists the Black woman's feminism is inseparable from the struggle for racial equality.
- [Negro Women in Our Economic Life](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/negro_women.md): Sadie T.M. Alexander (1930), *Opportunity*.
  Alexander argues that Black women's entry into manufacturing during and after World War I — nearly doubling their industrial workforce between 1910 and 1920 — reshaped both family income and the wider economy, helping make mass production possible. Drawing on census data and Women's Bureau surveys, she contends that factory work, despite low wages and marginal status, raised Black women's self-respect, broadened educational horizons, and offered a route to interracial working-class solidarity.

## Health and Populations

- [A Review of Hoffman's *Race Traits*](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/race_traits.md): Kelly Miller (1897), *American Negro Academy*.
  Miller dismantles Frederick L. Hoffman's widely cited Race Traits and Tendencies of the American Negro, which used Prudential Insurance mortality and census data to predict Black extinction from inherent "race traits" rather than social conditions. Miller shows that Hoffman's conclusions were reached a priori and then buttressed with selectively marshaled vital statistics, and counters with his own reading of the census to argue that Black mortality, morbidity, and crime track environment and post-Emancipation circumstance, not racial destiny.
- [The South and the Health of Negroes](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/health.md): Monroe N. Work (1915), *Southern Sociological Congress*.
  Work makes an explicitly economic case for Black health, estimating that sickness and preventable deaths among Southern Black residents cost the region roughly $200 million annually—more than the combined revenues of eight Southern states—and that tuberculosis alone costs $140 million, enough to capitalize 3,500 factories. Using city revenue data from Birmingham, Mobile, Montgomery, Savannah, and Charleston, he argues that a Black person is now worth five times his slavery-era valuation and that sanitary investment would double Black labor efficiency.
- [Enumeration Errors in Negro Population](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/enumeration.md): Kelly Miller (1922), *The Scientific Monthly*.
  Miller mounts a statistical attack on the 1920 Census, arguing that its reported 6.5 percent decennial growth in the Black population is an undercount of roughly 300,000—comparable to the errors the Bureau itself conceded for 1870 and 1890—rather than evidence of a declining Black birth rate. Comparing decade-over-decade rates, registration-area birth and death statistics, and reports from Oklahoma City enumerators, he warns that flawed federal data will be weaponized by "calamity philosophers" predicting Black racial decline.

## Social Movements

- [After Garvey---What?](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/after_garvey.md): Charles S. Johnson (1923), *Opportunity*.
  Writing just after Marcus Garvey's 1923 mail-fraud conviction, Johnson argues that jailing the man will not touch the movement, because Garveyism is a symptom of the peasant Black masses' repressed racial consciousness and their need for compensatory dreams of self-rule. Reading Garvey's UNIA speeches, the Black Star Line's finances, and the West Indian color hierarchy that shaped his hatred of the near-white, Johnson warns that the underlying unrest, blocked from fantasy, will seek another outlet.
- [The Negro Problem as Viewed by Negro Leaders](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/problem.md): Abram L. Harris (1923), *Current History*.
  Harris maps the landscape of Black leadership in the early 1920s, tracing the lineage from Frederick Douglass through Booker T. Washington's Atlanta Compromise to Du Bois's Niagara Movement and the NAACP, then out to the Messenger's socialist wing under Randolph and Owen, the Urban League, and Garvey's UNIA. Drawing on speeches, organizational platforms, and the Messenger's editorials, he argues that no single philosophy commands Black thought and classifies Garvey as a reactionary rather than a radical.
- [The Garvey Movement](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/garvey.md): E. Franklin Frazier (1926), *Opportunity*.
  Frazier reads the Garvey movement as a mass phenomenon whose power lay not in the feasibility of its African repatriation scheme but in its social-psychological machinery—uniforms, titles, a Black god, and the figure of Garvey as Moses and Christ—that gave ego-support to people denied standing in American life. Drawing on observation of UNIA ritual, comparison to evangelical revivals, and attention to the West Indian roots of Garvey's secular temper, he argues that urbanization and wartime ferment made the Black masses fertile ground for crowd leadership the NAACP and Urban League could not supply.
- [Tactics and Programs of Minority Groups](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/critical_analysis.md): Ralph J. Bunche (1935), *Journal of Negro Education*.
  Bunche surveys the tactical repertoire of Black civil-rights organizations between the wars — Garveyite separatism, Gandhian boycott, "don't buy where you can't work" campaigns, Booker T. Washington-style economic separatism, and the NAACP's court-based civil libertarianism — and finds each inadequate to a problem rooted in capitalist class structure rather than race alone. Drawing on Supreme Court decisions including Plessy, Grovey v. Townsend, and the Scottsboro cases, he argues that only a multiracial working-class movement can deliver real change.

## Methods

- [Program for a Sociological Society](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/program.md): W.E.B. Du Bois (1897), *First Sociological Club.*.
  Addressing the First Sociological Club, Du Bois argues that sociology is the child of the nineteenth-century industrial revolution and must become a rigorous empirical science of human action rather than drifting, reform-minded conversation. He lays out a six-part program tracing what sociology is, what it has accomplished, its methods, and how a local club should organize itself to gather facts on its own community, prefiguring the field-study model he would soon deploy in Philadelphia and Atlanta.
- [Study of the Negro Problems](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/negro_problems.md): W.E.B. Du Bois (1898), *The Annals*.
  Du Bois reframes "the Negro problem" as a plexus of distinct social problems that have evolved across 275 years of American history, from colonial labor regulation through the slave code, the Black Code, the plantation system, and Emancipation. He calls for systematic scientific study organized around historical, statistical, anthropological, and sociological methods, and argues that American universities and learned societies have a special obligation to underwrite this research as a condition of serious social knowledge.

## Crime

- [The Convict Lease System](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/convict.md): Ida B. Wells (1893), *Reason Why the Colored American Is Not in the World's Columbian Exposition*.
  Wells argues that the convict-lease system and lynch law are twin outgrowths of Southern class legislation that convert Black prisoners into a new form of chattel slavery while stripping them of the franchise. Drawing on Georgia prison statistics, George W. Cable's Prison Congress paper, and press reports from Tennessee coal mines and Mississippi convict farms, she documents sentences wildly disproportionate to petty offenses, sexual abuse by guards, and the hanging of a thirteen-year-old girl and a ten-year-old boy.
- [The Negro and Crime](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/negro_crime.md): W.E.B. Du Bois (1899), *The Independent*.
  Writing from Atlanta in the wake of the Sam Hose lynching, Du Bois insists that the Black criminal class represents neither the mass of the race nor any inherent disposition, and identifies four social causes of Southern Black crime: the convict-lease system, a double standard of justice in the courts, the escalating barbarity of lynch mobs, and the rigid color line that severs communication between the best elements of both races. He marshals editorial clippings from the Atlanta Constitution to show white murderers walking free while Black children draw chain-gang sentences for petty theft.
- [Crime Among the Negroes of Chicago: A Social Study](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/chicago_crime.md): Monroe N. Work (1900), *American Journal of Sociology*.
  Work uses Eleventh Census prisoner counts, Chicago police-court records, and fieldwork in the South Side levee district and the West Side's seventeenth and eighteenth wards to show that Black arrest rates in Chicago far exceed both white rates in the city and Black rates in the South. He locates the cause not in racial disposition but in the concentration of recent migrants in slum districts abandoned by the Black church and untouched by settlement houses, and in the narrow band of menial occupations open to Black workers.
- [Spawn of Slavery](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/spawn.md): W.E.B. Du Bois (1901), *Missionary Review*.
  Du Bois argues that the convict-lease system is the direct child of slavery, installed after Emancipation through vagrancy laws, hostile courts, and the auctioning of prisoners' labor to private lessees for state revenue. Drawing on New York State Library prison statistics showing the Southern states as the only region where prison income exceeded expense in 1890, together with Governor Atkinson's Georgia investigation and firsthand reports from camps such as Camp Hardmont, he traces the system's destruction of courts, free labor, and juvenile offenders alike.
- [What Negroes Think of Crime](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/think.md): W.E.B. Du Bois (1904), *Some Notes on Negro Crime*.
  Du Bois reports on a survey of roughly 1,500 Black schoolchildren in the Atlanta public schools and 534 older students drawn from across Georgia, asking them what laws, courts, policemen, and the chain gang are for. The responses reveal widespread distrust of police and courts, sympathy rather than disdain for chain-gang prisoners, and a shared conviction that the system brutalizes juvenile offenders; students attribute Black criminality to idleness, lack of home training, employment discrimination, and race prejudice, and call for reformatories and fair trials.
- [Crime Among Negroes](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/crime.md): Kelly Miller (1909), *The Southern Workman*.
  Drawing on the 1890 census and 1904 prison bulletins, Miller argues that the disproportionate Black share of U.S. prisoners reflects condition, not color: Black Americans constitute the urban submerged class where crime concentrates worldwide. He further contends that unequal Southern courts inflate Black commitments while shielding white offenders, citing the implausibly low white prison counts in Mississippi against Massachusetts as evidence of selective enforcement.
- [The Northern Negro and Crime](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/north_crime.md): Monroe N. Work (1910), *The Southern Workman*.
  Work mounts a methodological critique of crime statistics used against Black Northerners, arguing that arrest and conviction counts cannot measure criminality without accounting for the complexity of urban legal codes, the selectivity of police enforcement, and the historical legacy of slave-era laws that defined Black assertion as crime. Citing Pennsylvania Board of Public Charities data and an Independent article on Joliet, he reframes Northern Black imprisonment as a function of poverty and unequal legal representation.
- [Negro Criminality in the South](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/criminality.md): Monroe N. Work (1913), *The Annals*.
  Work traces Southern Black imprisonment from Emancipation through 1904, arguing that what appears as rising Black criminality is largely an artifact of the convict-lease system, which gave states a financial interest in convicting Black men. Using census prison counts, 1882-1912 lynching data, and commitment rates for rape, he shows Black commitment rates were lower than those for several European immigrant groups, and identifies the lease system, contract-labor laws, and liquor as the principal drivers of Southern Black crime.
- [The Negro Goes to Sing Sing](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/sing_sing.md): Ira De A. Reid (1932), *Opportunity*.
  Reid documents the rising share of Black admissions to New York's Sing Sing prison — from 11 percent in 1921 to nearly 24 percent by 1931 — and insists the pattern reflects social conditions, not racial criminality. Drawing on Warden Lewis Lawes's commitment records and his own case study of eighty Black inmates admitted in 1930, Reid ties the overrepresentation to unemployment, broken homes, and bad leisure habits rooted in the Great Migration's urban dislocations.
- [Juvenile Delinquency](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/juvenile_delinquency.md): E. Franklin Frazier (1932), *Negro Family in Chigago*.
  Frazier argues that rising Black juvenile delinquency in Chicago reflects neighborhood conditions rather than racial character: migrant families crowded into deteriorating zones near the Loop inherited delinquency rates that had been high for thirty years regardless of who lived there. Drawing on Juvenile Court data, Institute for Juvenile Research arrest records, and case documents, he traces a sharp gradient from over 40 percent delinquency in the innermost zone to under 2 percent in the stable, home-owning seventh zone.
- [Delinquency in the Negro Community](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/delinquency.md): Earl R. Moses (1933), *Opportunity*.
  Moses rejects the "homogeneous Black Belt" stereotype, arguing that Chicago's Black South Side contains sharply distinct areas of disorganization, transition, and organized community — and that delinquency maps onto this structure rather than onto race. Analyzing 1929 Juvenile Court records for 403 Black male delinquents by mile-zone and community type, he shows disorganized zones near the Loop produced delinquency rates six to seven times those of stable communities like West Woodlawn and Morgan Park.
- [Rebellious Youth](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/rebellious_youth.md): E. Franklin Frazier (1939), *Negro Family in the US*.
  Frazier argues that disproportionate delinquency among Black urban youth springs from the collapse of family and communal controls that followed Great Migration settlement in the most deteriorated zones of Northern and Southern cities. Drawing on juvenile-court records from Nashville, New York's Harlem, and Chicago, together with case documents from probation officers and mothers, he shows delinquency rates falling sharply as one moves outward from the urban core into zones of stable home-owning Black families.

## Education

- [Schools and the Vocational Life of Negroes](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/schools.md): Elise Johnson McDougald (1923), *Opportunity*.
  McDougald argues that Black children are systematically underserved by both Southern state systems and Northern public trade schools, and that vocational guidance must bridge school and workplace to counter the forces pushing Black workers into unskilled labor. Drawing on a New York City study showing that fewer than thirty percent of nearly two thousand Black workers used trade training and none had graduated from public trade schools, she reports on a four-year Board of Education counseling experiment in Harlem elementary schools.
- [The Etiquette of Race Relations](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/etiquette.md): Bertram W. Doyle (1936), *The Journal of Negro Education*.
  Doyle argues that the elaborate "etiquette" of Southern race relations — forms of address, hat-tipping, rules about who sits and who stands, who walks on the inside of the walk — functioned as a system of informal social control more binding than law, inherited from slavery and adapted into Jim Crow. Drawing on antebellum travelers' accounts, slave narratives, legal records, and period literature, he treats ceremonial observance as the machinery by which the color line was lubricated and maintained.
- [Negro Elementary School and the Cultural Pattern](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/pattern.md): Horace Mann Bond (1940), *Journal of Educational Sociology*.
  Writing as a newly installed college president rather than a detached scholar, Bond argues that Black elementary schools in the South must work within, but also push against, a caste-class structure of racial subordination and regional poverty that makes Mississippi apply tax rates thirteen times California's to fund comparable schooling. He draws on recent sociology — Dollard, Warner, Davis, Doyle, Frazier — to insist that the schoolmarm tradition of faith in the "improvability of mankind" remains indispensable even when social science counsels despair.

## Family

- [Three Scourges of the Negro Family](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/scourges.md): E. Franklin Frazier (1926), *Opportunity*.
  Frazier argues that family disorganization, poverty, and poor health compound one another in Black life, with the fragility of the family— inherited from slavery's destruction of African kinship and strained anew by low wages, migration, and the mass employment of Black women— lying at the root. Drawing on 1920 census occupational data, Women's Bureau figures from Jacksonville, Metropolitan Life mortality statistics, and his own rural Alabama survey where two-thirds of families lacked fathers, he insists that "socialization" through a stabilized home must precede other reforms.
- [Is the Negro Family a Unique Sociological Unit?](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/unique.md): E. Franklin Frazier (1927), *Opportunity*.
  Frazier argues that the Black family is not a biologically or culturally distinct sociological unit comparable to the Chinese lineage system but rather a group whose peculiarities stem from slavery's destruction of African kinship and from the economic and social conditions of American life. Drawing on cultural-analysis methods, 1920 census data on the concentration of Black workers in domestic service and agriculture, and attention to plantation conditions and racial intermixture, he contends the Black family warrants special sociological study because of historical rupture, not inherent uniqueness.
- [Negro Personality Changes in a Southern Community](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/community.md): Charles S. Johnson (1934), *Race and Culture Contacts*.
  Johnson argues that Black rural Alabamans are neither culturally African nor fully assimilated to Euro-American norms but have developed their own coherent folk conventions — around marriage, illegitimacy, divorce by "giving a strip," child adoption, and the matrifocal family — as functional adaptations to the plantation tenant system. Based on fieldwork with 612 Black families in an Alabama cotton county, he identifies home ownership, migration, and education as the forces now breaking down this accommodated order.

## Religion

- [Let Us Prey!](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/prey.md): Ira De A. Reid (1926), *Opportunity*.
  Reid's ethnographic survey of 140 Harlem churches within a 150-block radius distinguishes the established institutional churches—Abyssinian Baptist, St. Philip's, Mother A.M.E. Zion—from a proliferating underbrush of storefront and "house" churches, spiritualist cults, Black Jewish congregations, and self-anointed bishops he characterizes as religious exploiters. Tabulating denominations, rental arrangements, and the signage and liturgies of specific cults such as the Temple of the Gospel of the Kingdom, he argues that economic precarity and unregulated ministerial entrepreneurship have prostituted the Harlem church.

## Optional

- [PDF scans](https://crisisopportunity.org/articles/PDFS/): original page images for each article where available
